perturbation.original_text: INSURANCE The Company agrees to provide and maintain, at its own expense, advertising and product liability insurance each with limits no less than $5,000,000 and within thirty (30) days from the date hereof, the Company will submit to Nantz Communications a fully paid policy or certificate of insurance naming Nantz Communications and Nantz as insured parties, requiring that the insurer shall not terminate or materially modify such without written notice to Nantz Communications at least twenty (20) days in advance thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: INSURANCE The Company agrees to provide and maintain, at its own expense, advertising and product liability insurance each with limits no less than $5,000,000 and within sixty (60) days from the date hereof, the Company will submit to Nantz Communications a fully paid policy or certificate of insurance naming Nantz Communications and Nantz as insured parties, requiring that the insurer shall not terminate or materially modify such without written notice to Nantz Communications at least ten (10) days in advance thereof.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the advance notice period required for insurance policy modifications or termination from twenty (20) days to ten (10) days, which may conflict with California Insurance Code regulations regarding notification periods.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Insurance Code § 678.1 generally requires insurers to provide a minimum of 30 days' advance notice for policy cancellations or non-renewals. Reducing the notification period to ten (10) days contradicts this requirement, making the contractual clause potentially unenforceable under California law. While this section does not directly address modifications, the general principle of adequate notice before changes to coverage is well-established and could be argued in court.

GENERAL RULES GOVERNING INSURANCE [100 - 1879.8] ( Division 1 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) PART 1. THE CONTRACT [100 - 679.75] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1935, Ch. 145. ) CHAPTER 11. Cancellation and Failure to Renew Certain Property Insurance [675 - 679.7] ( Chapter 11 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 313. ) 678.1. (a) This section applies only to policies of insurance of commercial insurance that are subject to Sections 675.5 and 676.6. (b) A notice of nonrenewal shall be in writing and shall be delivered or mailed to the producer of record and to the named insured at the mailing address shown on the policy. The time periods and procedures in subdivision (a) of Section 1013 of the Code of Civil Procedure shall be applicable if the notice is mailed. (c) An insurer, at least 60 days, but not more than 120 days, in advance of the end of the policy period, shall give notice of nonrenewal, and the reasons for the nonrenewal, if the insurer intends not to renew the policy, or to condition renewal upon reduction of limits, elimination of coverages, increase in deductibles, or increase of more than 25 percent in the rate upon which the premium is based. (d) If an insurer fails to give timely notice required by subdivision (c), the policy of
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the advance notice period for insurance policy modifications from 20 days to 10 days. The Law Explanation says that California Insurance Code § 678.1 generally requires insurers to provide a minimum of 30 days' advance notice for policy cancellations or non-renewals, and that reducing the notification period to 10 days contradicts this requirement. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing California Insurance Code - INS DIVISION 1, details notice of nonrenewal requirements. The modification directly conflicts with the general principle of adequate notice before changes to coverage, establishing a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 23. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of this Agreement after being given notice thereof and, where applicable, a reasonable opportunity to cure the failure (which shall be 10 days in the event of a failure to timely make a payment pursuant hereto; 30 days otherwise).
perturbation.changed_text: 23. Failure to comply with the terms and conditions of this Agreement after being given notice thereof and, where applicable, a reasonable opportunity to cure the failure (which shall be 3 days in the event of a failure to timely make a payment pursuant hereto; 15 days otherwise).
perturbation.explanation: The modification reduces the cure period for payment failures from 10 days to 3 days, which may conflict with California laws regarding payment of wages and breach of contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 204 dictates payment schedules and methods, and while it doesn't explicitly define a 'cure period,' reducing it to 3 days could be argued as an unreasonable timeframe to resolve payment issues, especially if the error originates with the company. Additionally, a breach of contract claim generally allows for more than 3 days to resolve before escalating to legal action. Shortening this period could be deemed unconscionable.

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 204. (a) All wages, other than those mentioned in Section 201, 201.3, 202, 204.1, or 204.2, earned by any person in any employment are due and payable twice during each calendar month, on days designated in advance by the employer as the regular paydays. Labor performed between the 1st and 15th days, inclusive, of any calendar month shall be paid for between the 16th and the 26th day of the month during which the labor was performed, and labor performed between the 16th and the last day, inclusive, of any calendar month, shall be paid for between the 1st and 10th day of the following month. However, salaries of executive, administrative, and professional employees of employers covered by the Fair Labor Standards Act, as set forth pursuant to Section 13(a)(1) of the Fair Labor Standards Act, as amended through March 1, 1969, in Part 541 of Title 29 of the Code of Federal Regulations, as that p
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the cure period for payment failures from 10 days to 3 days. The original text provided a 10-day cure period for payment failures, while the modified text reduces this to 3 days. According to the perturbation explanation, this may conflict with California laws. The law explanation states that reducing it to 3 days could be argued as an unreasonable timeframe to resolve payment issues. California Labor Code § 204, as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, dictates payment schedules and methods. While it doesn't explicitly define a 'cure period,' the reduced timeframe could be deemed unconscionable.

perturbation.original_text: 24. NOTICES. Any and all notices required pursuant to this Agreement shall be deemed given if in writing and delivered in person, sent by certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, or set by telefax at or to the addresses and telefax numbers set forth below or such other addresses and telefax numbers as the parties may direct by notice given as herein provided:
perturbation.changed_text: 24. NOTICES. Any and all notices required pursuant to this Agreement shall be deemed given if delivered via carrier pigeon only to the addresses set forth below or such other addresses as the parties may direct by notice given as herein provided:
perturbation.explanation: The modification restricts the acceptable methods of delivering notices to only delivery by carrier pigeon, removing options such as certified or registered mail, return receipt requested, or telefax. This could conflict with standard legal practices and expectations for providing adequate notice.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1013 outlines methods for service of notices and papers in legal proceedings. While it doesn't explicitly mandate specific methods for contractual notices, it establishes acceptable standards for ensuring proper notice, such as mail. Restricting notice to carrier pigeon only is unreasonable and likely unenforceable, as it's unreliable, lacks proof of delivery, and conflicts with the spirit of ensuring adequate notification.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1013 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 5. Notices, and Filing and Service of Papers [1010 - 1020] ( Chapter 5 enacted 1872. ) 1013. (a) In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office, mailbox, subpost office, substation, or mail chute, or other like facility regularly maintained by the United States Postal Service, in a sealed envelope, with postage paid, addressed to the person on whom it is to be served, at the office address as last given by that person on any document filed in the cause and served on the party making service by mail; otherwise at that party’s place of residence. Service is complete at the time of the deposit, but any period of notice and any right or duty to do any act or make any response within any period or on a date certain after service of the document, which time period or date is prescribed by statute or rule of court, shall be extended five calendar days, upon service by mail, if the place of address and the place of mailing is within the State of California, 10 calendar days if either the place of mailing or the place of address is outside the State of California
perturbation.justification: The modified text drastically restricts acceptable methods of delivering notices to only delivery by carrier pigeon, removing standard options such as certified or registered mail. This directly contradicts the spirit, if not the letter, of ensuring adequate notification as described in the Law Explanation. While CCP 1013 does not mandate specific methods for contractual notices, it does establish acceptable standards for ensuring proper notice, such as mail. By eliminating mail as an option, the modification introduces an unreliable method lacking proof of delivery which undermines the standards for providing adequate notification contemplated by CCP 1013(a), 'In case of service by mail, the notice or other paper shall be deposited in a post office...'. The modified contract would likely fail legal scrutiny because it provides a method of notification that is wholly insufficient when compared to accepted legal standards.
